---
title: "Summaries"
author: "Robert Esposito & Kendra Dickinson, Ph.D."
format: pdf
---

# Article Summaries

Note: If I can't find the article yet, I just put the abstract. 

## @stephens1989language

Portuguese in USA. Arrivals began in 1820s, picked up in 1830s with arrival fo Lusitanian and Azorean. Congregated to New England, specifically MA. 1850s, to California (San Fran).

Late 19th/early 20th century, big influx to CA, MA, NY, & NJ. Mid-1970s, major immigration event.

Early immigrants = high illiteracy, low English.

Azorean Portuguese is stigmatized [@middleton1986language].

Began arriving to Newark in large groups in 1920s [@cunha1981portugueses].

Bilingual programs (I guess school programs?) & Sunday schools. 15 private Portuguese schools, similar to Hebrew schools.

## @carvalho2010portuguese

Portuguese immigrants mainly from Azores Islands, mainland Portugual, and Cape Verdean, smaller population from Mozambique and Angola. High concentrations in MA. Over last 20 years, Cape Verdeans & Brazilians account for most PT speakers.

14,829 students taking Portuguese classes in K-12 schools in 2008 [@vicente2008promoccao].

Modern Language Association in 2006 reported 22.4% increase of availability of Portuguese instruction between 2002 and 2006.

US Census underestimates PT speakers in USA. Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Brazil report much higher estimate. Due to undocumented immigrants.

National Bilingual Education Act of 1968 resulted in PT-EN bilingual education initiatives in 1970s.

Republic of Cape Verde immigrants speak a Portuguese-based creole and Portuguese. They typically are more receptive bilinguals than e.g., Azorean or Madeiran speakers in the US (Ferreira 2005; need to find bibtex). Cape Verdean-Americans tend to be L1 Crole L1 EN/L2 EN, and then may learn PT as L3 in school.

Brazilian immigration in 1984-1987, again in 1990s. Young, educated, middle-class men and women on tourist visas and overstayed (Bensabat-Ott 2000; need to find bibtex). Later, working class immigrants from small towns and rural areas who entered via US-Mexico border.

Luso-American Foundation (FLAD, Fundacao Luso-Americana de Desenvolvimento) identified `r 52 + 25 + 20 + 17 + 13 + 10 + 5 +1` K-12 public and community schools that offer Portuguese. 20 in NJ, 10 in NY, 13 in CT; weekend Portuguese language schools in NJ [@vicente2008promoccao].

Dual-immersion EN-PT program in K-8 school in MA resulted in greater awareness of dialectal differences among teachers, parents, and staff [@rubinstein2002problematizing].

Dialectal differences do not result in communicative breakdown between students (Rubinstein-Avila, 2002; Ferreira, 2005).

## @osborne2021perception

Mid vowel contrasts as the last to be acquired by L1 speakers (Matzenauer & Costa, 2017). Low functional load with few minimal pairs (Alves, 2008).

12 BR-PT heritage speakers. Completed BLP (all except one was English-dominant, but pretty large range).

AXB discrimination. Check article for list of stimuli.

2AFC identification task.

Heritage speakers accurately categorize PT mid vowels, but higher EN dominance predicted more trouble with /ɔ/-/o/. Amengual (2015) found the same asymmetry for ES-Catalan bilinguals. 

## @rothman2014portuguese

No studies examining Cape Verdean Portuguese as a heritage language. (Although, as noted by @carvalho2010portuguese, they would be learning Cape Verdean Creole at home).

PT-PT stops are pronounced as fricatives; BR-PT pronounces as plosives. Would be interesting to see if PT-PT maintains fricatives in Newark given input from BR-PT and English.

For our reference, this chapter also includes presents demographic information (though somewhat outdated, from 2000, 2008, etc.) on Portuguese speakers in the United States, including population size, growth over time, and age distribution. It also details ancestry groups (Portuguese, Brazilian, Cape Verdean) and their regional concentration across U.S. regions and states, along with data on English proficiency as an indicator of bilingualism and language shift. 

Summary of heritage speakers learning in college, focused on dialectal differences [@silva2010starting].

## @silva2010starting

Developing a heritage speaker track in college for PT. Focus on dialectal differences.

## @galves2005syntax
Syntactic position of clitic pronouns (e.g., *me, te, se, o, lhe*) in EP and BP arise from both syntactic and morphophonological factors. The authors argue that EP clitics attach to Infl (inflectional category) and BP clitics attach to the verb (V) in the syntactic structure. Additionally, the authors argue that EP does not permit clitics in sentence-initial position, whereas BP does not have this restriction. This results in a strong preference for enclisis (post-verbal clitic) in EP (*chamou-me*), and a preference for proclisis (pre-verbal clitic) in BP (*me chamou*). In multi-verb constructions, EP clitics attach to the finite verb (*tinham-se entendido perfeitamente*), while in BP they tend to attach to the non-finite verb (*tinham se entendido perfeitamente*)

## @amadeo2025perception

Escudero et al. (2009; "A cross-dialect acoustic description of vowels: Brazilian and European Portuguese) and Rato & Carlet (2020) show that BR-PT and PT-PT share some similarities in their vowel inventory.

Diaz-Granado (2011) found that /e ~ ɛ/ in English & Portuguese are not similar, so L1 En L2 Pt are expected to collapse PT /e ~ ɛ/ into a single category. Tested L1 En L2 Pt and L1 En L2 Es L3 Pt. L3 learners performed better. But there were only 12 in the first group, 5 in the second.

Kendall (2004) tested 20 beginning and advanced L1 En L2 Pt learners on BP mid vowels. They did poorly.

Vasiliev (2013) tested L1 English L2 Pt stressed oral vowels. /e ~ ɛ/ was second-easiest.

Elvin et al. (2018) L1 En monolinguals, En-Es bilinguals tested on discrimination task for Pt-Pt and Pt-Br. /o ~ ɔ/ easier in EP than BP, which was second-easiest to discriminate. Participants had more trouble with /i ~ e/ and /u ~ o/.

Summary: For L1 US En, /e ɛ o c/ are hardest to discriminate; /e ~ ɛ, o ~ ɔ, e ~ i, o ~ u/ are most challenging minimal pairs. These challenges are accurately predicted by perception models e.g., Escudero 2009.

NEED TO FINISH THIS SUMMARY AND ALSO FIND ALL OF THESE ARTICLES.

## @albano2021some

Review of laboratory phonology of Portuguese.

Vowel Variability: 

-   /a ɐ/: In EP.Northern EP losing /a ɐ/ distinction in stressed syllables in perception. (From [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portuguese_phonology): "In central European Portuguese this contrast occurs in a limited morphological context, namely in verb conjugation between the first person plural present and past perfect indicative forms of verbs such as pensamos ('we think') and pensámos ('we thought'; spelled ⟨pensamos⟩ in Brazil)." I would assume this contrast gets lost pretty quickly in diaspora.

-   Vowel-to-vowel interactions across syllables (metaonymy; vowel harmony). Morphologized influence of a truncated theme vowel on mid stem vowels of the second and third conjugations, as in 'sinto' vs 'sente'.

-   Height harmony in Rio e.g., m[i]nino, f[u]linha.

-   Vowel lowering: higher F1 for [e o] before stressed low-mid vowels.

Stress & Prosodic Grouping:

-   BP stress acoustically predictable from spectrum, not duration nor F0 [@harmath2021detecting]. What cues do heritage PT speakers use for stress? If spectral features are important for stress, we might predict they maintain those vowel distinctions; or, we might predict that they lose the spectral features and pick up different cues for stress (e.g., F0, duration, amplitude).

-   Antepenultimate, penultimate, and final stress are not equally recognizable [@martins1988ouvir]. Penultimate is most frequent and most recognizable; followed by final and antepenultimate. Differs in L1 child acquisition: 5-6 month old EP-learning infants prefer iambic to trochaic stress (opposite of adults), and they pay attention to visual (facial) cues for stress [@cruz2020eyes]. 

-   BP phonological versus morphological word [@camara1992estrutura]. Related to Prosodic Phonology (Nespor & Vogel).

-   Syllable duration and F0 variation as uces for distinguishing prosodic status of prepositional clitic-host sequences from prosodic word sequences in BP [@da2021prosodization]. Phonetic realization of unstressed syllables at prosoidc word boundaries. Duration does not distinguish prosodic word from the clitic prosodization sequences. Does F0 variation signal an intonation associated to clitic-host sequences distinct from prosodic word sequences?

Nasality:

-   Nasal vowels versus nasalized vowels (resulting from coarticulation). Velum opening is gradient, not binary?

Fricative variability:

## @harmath2021detecting

Stress.

## @camara1992estrutura

Book on BP prosody.

## @da2021prosodization

Clitics and prosody. Seems related to @galves2005syntax.

## @martins1988ouvir

Stress perception.

## @cruz2020eyes

L1 acquisition. Eye-tracking. Stress perception.

# Topic Summaries

## Clitics

@galves2005syntax

## Portuguese in Newark

@stephens1989language
@carvalho2010portuguese

## /R/

### Production

### Perception

## Mid vowels

### Production

### Perception

@osborne2021perception

## Stress

### Production

### Perception

## Stops

@rothman2014portuguese

### Production

### Perception

# Misc.

Fernanda A. Ferreira has a lot of research on heritage speakers in college classes.
